observations.recommendation:
To enhance asset management and address the noted deficiencies, the following
measures are recommended, considering the agency’s operating realities:
a. Implement an Asset Management Framework by developing and
institutionalizing an Asset Management Manual that is aligned with the
aforementioned COA Circulars and accounting policies. The manual should
include roles and responsibilities, specific procedures/processes, timelines,
control mechanisms, and sanctions for non-compliance.
b. Reassess manpower complement in the CGSO and match it with the existing
operational and regulatory demands and workloads. Also, implement a
capacity- building program that includes regular trainings relevant to asset
management and financial reporting.
c. Review the lapsing schedule and recognize the accumulated depreciation for
PPEs with acquisition cost of ₱50,000.00 and above that are still serviceable to
ensure accurate carrying values. For semi-expendable items, reclassify them
to Government Equity account, as these were issued in the previous years.
Effect the necessary adjusting journal entries to properly reflect the carrying
value of PPEs in the books.
d. Conduct the one-time cleansing of PPEs to identify and remove non-existence
assets from CGTs books, to ensure that only properly accounted and verifiable
assets remain in the records, improving the accuracy and reliability of
financial reporting.

To address the noted deficiency, we recommended that Management:
a. Instruct the City Accountant to prepare the necessary adjusting journal
entries to correct the error on recording in Due to Other Fund – GF as of
December 31, 2024.
b. Install a control mechanism to be embedded within the reporting process to
ensure that balances of reciprocal accounts are reconciled and any
discrepancy detected shall be immediately resolved or adjusted to maintain
the accuracy and reliability of account balances.

To address the identified deficiencies and mitigate the associated risks, we
recommended that Management implement the following actions:
a. Strengthen the internal control system by documenting and implementing
specific control activities related to cash management, in accordance with the
NGAS Manual and related policies.
b. Direct all collecting officers to remit collections in full and prepare the RCD
at the close of each business day to ensure complete and accurate recording
and reporting of transactions by the Accounting Office.
c. Instruct the OIC-City Treasurer to implement stricter oversight of collecting
officers handling cash tickets through continuous, periodic surprise cash
counts and reconciliations, as detailed in the documented control activities.
d. Ensure that reconciliations of cash balances are conducted between the
accountable officer and the accountant at least quarterly, or more frequently
if required by management.
e. Conduct training sessions for all employees involved in cash management to
ensure they understand the new policies and procedures. Distribute the Cash
Management Manual to all relevant staff and make it accessible for reference.

To address these observations and mitigate associated risks, we recommended
that Management take the following actions:
a. Enforce strict liquidation policies by requiring all accountable officers to settle
outstanding cash advances within a reasonable period and suspending new
cash advances for officers and employees with overdue balances until full
liquidation is achieved.
b. Enhance recovery of advances from separated employees by issuing demand
letters and pursuing legal action if necessary. Strengthen exit clearance
procedures to ensure all financial obligations are settled before approval,
holding the approving official accountable. Request COA write-offs only after
exhausting all recovery efforts.
c. Enhance training and capacity building by conducting regular training on PD
1445 and COA Circulars; and holding accountability seminars to underscore
the legal and fiscal repercussions of non-compliance.
d. Implement disciplinary measures by imposing sanctions, such as salary holds
or formal reprimands, for delayed liquidations and incorporating compliance
with cash advance policies into performance evaluations and promotions.
e. Improve monitoring and reporting by tasking the Internal Audit Unit with
monthly reconciliation of cash advances and aging balance reporting and
considering public disclosure of liquidation statuses to enable public scrutiny
and enhance transparency.

To address the noted deficiencies and identified gaps, we recommended that
Management:
a. Enforce compliance with the accounting guidelines in accordance with the
NGAS Manual and strengthen the Inventory Management System by
documenting specific control activities, including roles and responsibilities,
step-by-step procedures/processes, timelines, control mechanisms, and
sanctions for non-compliance.
b. Consider upgrading the current accounting software to include the feature on
SLC maintenance and weighted average computation of inventory cost.
c. Reassess manpower complement in the CGSO and match it with the existing
operational and regulatory demands and workloads.
d. Instruct the OAIAS to coordinate with the offices in-charge in the fuel
consumption representing the reported Fuel, Oil, Lubricants Inventory as of
December 31, 2024 and require the submission of necessary supporting
documents to prompt the liquidation thereof and recognize the appropriate
adjusting entries.
e. Include in the Inventory Management Manual, the processes, necessary
documentation and control measures related to the receipt of inventories from
other agencies (i.e. DOH/ PHO) and eventual consumption or transfer thereof
to other agencies (LGU/ NGOs/POs).

To facilitate improvements in this area, the following recommendations were
provided for Management's consideration:
a. Develop and implement an Asset Management Framework by developing and
institutionalizing an Asset Management Manual that is aligned with the
aforementioned accounting and reporting guidelines. The Manual should
cover the roles and responsibilities, specific procedures/processes, timelines,
control mechanisms, and sanctions for non-compliance.
b. Address historical CIP items (2010-2024)
 Form a Technical Working Group to review long-standing CIP accounts
and recommend necessary adjustments.
 Develop a phased approach to clearing backlog CIP items, prioritizing
those with the most significant financial impact.
 Conduct the one-time cleansing of PPEs to identify and remove non-
existent CIPs, in accordance with the disposition procedures.
 Draw the proper journal entry vouchers, based on the above
reconciliations, to reclassify the CIP accounts to their corresponding PPE
and depreciation accounts. This is to reflect the correct book value thereof.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and management agreed that the LGUs of the province
further enhance the quality of its individual LDRRM management processes by
taking concrete actions to address the issues related to manpower, data and
information, and formalization to reinforce the establishment of an enabling level of
coordination in a collective setting and ensure disaster resiliency.

As regards the Bayanihan Funds/Grant that remained unutilized, we
recommended and management agreed that the CGT, in coordination with oversight
agencies, get back to the drawing board, and come up with a plan that would layout
initial efforts towards inclusive rehabilitation strategies, along with the sustained
management of the perils brought about by the COVID-19 pandemic.

Reinforcing the Community-Based Monitoring System as a Tool in Designing
Appropriate COVID-19 Interventions, and Comprehensive Monitoring and Analysis

It is the policy of the State to free the people from poverty through policies that
provide adequate social services, deliver a rising standard of living, promote full
employment, and make available an improved quality of life for all.12 Pursuant to this
policy, the State recognizes the need to adopt focused and specific measures that will
ensure poverty reduction wherein citizens have access to social protection and welfare
programs that address their minimum basic needs. The State further recognizes that a
system of public spending that warrants government allocation on areas and populace that
are most wanting is necessary in lifting people out of poverty.13

Towards this end, the State shall adopt a Community-Based Monitoring System
(CBMS) which generates updated and disaggregated data necessary in targeting
beneficiaries, conducting more comprehensive poverty analysis and needs prioritization,
designing appropriate policies and interventions, and monitoring impact over time.14
Moreover, the CBMS shall refer to an organized technology-based system of collecting,
processing and validating necessary disaggregated data that may be used for planning,
program implementation and impact monitoring at the local level while empowering
communities to participate in the process. It involves the generation of data at the local
level which serves as basis in targeting households in the planning, budgeting and
implementation of government programs geared towards poverty alleviation and economic
development. This system merges the methodologies used in data collection activities of
all national agencies, geo-tagging, and the CBMS implemented by the LGUs. It entails a
census of households undertaken by the LGUs with the participation of the community
using accelerated poverty profiling system, in the data collection, processing, mapping and
analysis of data.15

As forwarded by the foregoing narratives, it shall accordingly be incumbent upon
the LGUs to maintain their respective data bases for varied purposes. That such purposes
shall in no way be limited to identification purposes, but to afford the LGUs a better view
of its constituencies for a more inclusive approach towards the rendering of public services.
That the data management system likewise plays an essential piece in the entire life cycle
of the program management, that is from planning down to monitoring. That the
implementation of the response efforts relative to the COVID-19 is no exception to this,
considering that in this time of the pandemic, data accuracy and its reliability define the
extent of the effects of the disease, and spells the difference as to the levels and types of
interventions needed. For one, several issues have manifested with respect to the data
production for purposes of contact tracing, as apparently, data logs cannot be relied upon
as several names do not appear in the actual and available registry of the LGUs. Therefore,
contact tracing to this end would not be representative of the actual logs, thus the
compromised data accuracy may as well negatively bring a huge deal towards the health
and safety of the people in the locality.

We sought information from the management to this effect, and that highlighting
the vantage point, and as a feed-forward information, it was explained that the situation the
CGT is in, in view of the COVID-19 pandemic is basically new. However, as continued, it
was manifested that there has to be something done to improve specifically on the planning,
implementation and monitoring phases. We clarified further of said statements, and it was
forwarded that accordingly, the system of data banking which intends to immediately
respond to the possible local transmission of the pandemic, and the whereabouts of targeted
individuals and the clusters of population at risk essentially plays a key role in risks
management. Further, to support the earlier manifestations, management said that the
experiences the CGT had in targeting the priority population for the social amelioration
assistance, and that of the food assistance programs have led them to realize how a realistic
and an updated data would dictate the pacing of the implementation activities.

The audit team on its end, and taking off from the manifestations provided by the
management, has brought the latter’s attention to having a dynamic, realistic and updated
data base system concerning essential information for purposes of facilitating better and
comprehensive planning. That relative thereto, regular and synchronized data collection
shall be conducted by every city and municipality every three (3) years. In the conduct of
data collection, the LGU shall receive financial and technical assistance from the
appropriate national government agencies.16 Notwithstanding the preceding paragraph, all
cities and municipalities are enjoined to collect data at shorter intervals and at their own
expense for purposes peculiarly useful to them.17 Management on the affirmative, narrated
that the latest data available was that of the one gathered in the year 2015. But accordingly,
insofar as baselines and targets of priority clusters of population is concerned, the
management believes that they do have one, but is accordingly one that is in an ongoing
update, with the CBMS data collection activities recently having been culminated by the
CGT. The audit team supports this, as data collection by the CGT is coursed through and
headed by the City Planning and Development Office (CPDO), and to date is comprised of
few personnel. That for purposes of undertaking the CBMS, an adhoc team, for a limited
duration and specific purpose is usually being constituted comprising of several personnel
coming in from different offices. As explained by management, these limitations have kept
them at bay, but is however doing more with less resources. The audit team appreciates the
efforts taken, but we deemed it necessary to highlight that the time that had elapsed from
the year 2015, or approximately, five (5) years thereafter would translate to a differential
of information, that when taken into consideration would spell the difference. In response,
management added that, had the data been updated timely, it would somehow feed them
closer to responses with greater inclusivity.

We recommended and management agreed that the CGT, through the CPDO,
to:

a. hasten the collation of data resulting from the recently concluded CBMS
activities, and keep tab of the differential information for purposes of
determining significant changes from the old data base to facilitate
normalization of data;

b. formalize the creation of an adhoc CBMS team, when warranted, to ensure
that accountability is defined, and respective roles are made much clearer;
and

c. institutionalize the conduct of CBMS activities as frequent as possible, or
in closer intervals, with a view of the availability of resources or logistical
requirements needed to ensure a much more reliable data base in the
crafting of local development plans;

Formalizing and Codification of the Local Emergency Protocols and Structure

In line with the provisions of Republic Act No. 10121 otherwise known as the
Philippine Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Act of 2010, particularly Section 2
(a), which mandates the State to uphold the people’s Constitutional right to life and
property by addressing the root causes of vulnerabilities to disasters, strengthening the
country’s institutional capacity for disaster risk reduction and management and building
the resilience of local communities to disasters including climate change impacts, and in
consideration of the increasing frequency, severity and unpredictability of disaster
occurrences that the country has experienced for the past years, the DILG enjoins all local
chief executives to perform early preparedness actions to reduce disaster risks. Such actions
as suggested include among others the following: (a) creation of systems and structures;
(b) institutionalizing policies and plans; (c) building competencies; and (d) prepositioning
of equipment and supplies.18

Creation of Systems and Structures

Inherent in a well-functioning LGU is the existence of a well-built, and dynamic
emergency protocol, codified for ease of reference. For one, the existence of systems and
structures operate by providing on site guidance and direction to the management of
incidences. It shall accordingly cover on-scene management of emergency response
operations from disaster impact to the immediate emergency response phase of an incident,
with priority objective of saving lives, ensuring safety of responders and others, protecting
property and environment, and incident stabilization.19

The CGT is no exception to this as management yielded that at the onset and even
up to the present, the IATF (Inter Agency Task Force), and that of CGT’s Incident
Management Team (IMT) continue to work collaboratively. As continued, management
explained that the assessment processes were undertaken by the IATF with respect to the
LGU’s capability of mitigating the impact of the pandemic. While accordingly, the over-
all COVID-19 management is coursed through the IMT. We asked the management of
concerns and/or issues encountered vis-à-vis existing set-up, and as forwarded from the
responses, it appeared that issues as regards timely coordination cropped-up along the way,
in particular the availability of food packers charged with sorting out of food items to
affected households and/or beneficiaries. However, management is quick to elaborate that
they had it remedied by tapping security forces and CGT employees. Management likewise
added that the very limited time in completing, and in the delivery of services had been
aggravated by work and mobility restrictions imposed in the locality. Thus, the availability
of readily available manpower had been a struggle then. We had a word with the
management on this, and we were told that the CGT was never dampened by the lack of
personnel at the onset of COVID-19 pandemic, as the spirit of community service is greatly
valued in the City, that afforded them the services of volunteers.

The audit team deeply appreciates the efforts of the CGT with respect to
management of disaster, but just like any other LGU, who had been caught almost empty-
handed by the COVID-19 pandemic, learning experiences then become opportunities to
improve on the aspects where gaps were noted. For one, we bring management’s attention
to the provisions contained under NDRRMC Memorandum Circular No. 02, s. 2012, which
provides the suggested set-up for the Incident Command System (ICS). Likewise, in
keeping with the DILG Memorandum Circular No. 2015-76, it shall be incumbent upon
the local chief executive concerned to issue an Executive Order establishing the ICS, or if
there is one, to cause the issuance of an updated directive, taking note of how an ideal ICS
organization should be. Relatively, in the constitution of an ICS, or an IMT for that matter,
we opined that the CGT should also institutionalize the creation of an adhoc and/or
volunteer teams purposely to work on tasks during the untoward circumstances such as the
COVID-19 pandemic. Likewise, the adhoc team or any of its equivalent, of course without
infringing the constitutional provision against forced labor, shall as much as possible be
composed of CGT employees. However, the CGT shall in no case be precluded from
involving volunteer organizations in the adhoc team. We also inquired on the manpower
complement of the CDRRMO, and that we noted how the said office maximized the
manpower it presently has, and due regard it placed on the efforts of quite a number of
individual volunteers. The guidelines however require that the respective LGUs fill-out the
plantilla positions of one (1) Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (LDRRM)
Officer, and three (3) DRRM staff pursuant to NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 2014-1 dated April 4, 2014.20 The CGT however has a
dedicated personnel and support staff charged with managing the affairs and functions of
said office, and is being backstopped by existing support arrangements. However, the CGT
has to explore on the possibility of expanding the personnel complement of the said office,
and shall have to assess the viability of the same vis-à-vis availability of budget relative to
the same initiative.

We recommended and management agreed that the CGT, in view of the
learning experiences brought about by the COVID-19 pandemic to:

a. revisit the existing incident and/or emergency support structures, update,
and incorporate changes that can well adapt to the changing
circumstances, and the pressing concerns brought about by the COVID-19
pandemic; and

b. assess the viability and availability of budget complement, and if
warranted to look into the possibility of beefing-up the manpower
complement of the LDRRM Office.

Institutionalizing Policies and Plans, and Building Competencies through Maximization of
the COVID-19 Learning Experiences

Pursuant to the implementing rules and regulations, the LGUs shall cause the
preparation, review, and/or updating of a Contingency Plan which, at the minimum,
includes scheme for (a) activation of the early warning system; (b) activation of pre-
designated evacuation centers; (c) evacuation routes/maps; (d) search and rescue; (e)
emergency response; (f) medical and counseling services; (g) distribution of relief goods;
and (h) evacuation center management.21 For the purpose of review, and due regard to the
management for the limitations brought about by the pandemic, we focused on the
emergency response and the distribution of relief goods, these being the key activities
undertaken at the onset of the pandemic.

At the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, the CGT among others had been
constrained by time in completing the works relative to the responses undertaken, coupled
with the lack of manpower and closures of business establishments brought about the office
and mobility restrictions imposed during the strictest level of community quarantine in the
country. The audit team has obtained a copy of the CGT’s Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Climate Change Adaptation Plan (DRRM-CCA) for 2014-2019.

Understandably, the same document having been prepared way ahead of the present
pandemic, that reading of the same manifested the focus accorded to climate change related
calamities. This now spoke of the need, not only by the CGT, but for the national
government to look into the bigger picture in the crafting of disaster management plans,
that the definition of disasters and/or calamities eventually has now come to change with
the advent of the COVID-19 pandemic.

The audit team further elicited information from the management as regards the
experiences encountered at the onset of the pandemic, and that we were informed that while
the CGT too has at some point grasping, it has somehow managed to work on doing what
needs to be done at its level. Additionally, the management further shared that hindrances
have at some point added to the difficulties such as the ones involving the dealings with
support agencies and private entities as accordingly, almost transactions and business
operations had shut down and/or were destabilized. Thus, as management said,
procurement of prime commodities and goods for the affected households and those
individuals catered in the various isolation facilities had been a struggle, and the escalation
of the surging demand against depleting stocks/supplies available in the locality, had the
CGT at bay at some point. All these have been valuable learning experiences for the CGT,
and that of the management, that deserves consideration in the crafting of disaster
management plans.

We looked further into the details of the DRRM-CCA of the CGT, and that we
noted of the efforts taken by the management in coming up with such document as
reference of actions. We managed to look into the definition of the roles of the agencies
and stakeholders involved, in that we were apprised of how the CGT worked on putting in
the responsibilities expected of the offices and agencies concerned in the various facets of
disaster management including the aspects of preparedness, prevention, mitigation,
rehabilitation and recovery. To backstop the observation raised as regards the institution of
systems and structure, the disaster management plan should likewise mirror the initiatives
of the LGUs insofar as the formalization of a more comprehensive disaster management
structure is concerned. The risk profile in the said document likewise should be updated to
include the learning experiences obtained at the onset and during the COVID-19 pandemic.
In this way, the disaster management plan would become a more dynamic document,
facilitative of actions that may be taken should there be situations alike that the CGT may
be facing in the future. Management explained that indeed succeeding actions will be taken
to update the disaster management plan, as accordingly, risks have evolved, that the
oversight agencies too, have noted of the evolving vulnerabilities.

We recommended and management agreed that the CGT, in view of the
learning experiences brought about by the COVID-19 pandemic to:

a. initiate the updating of the disaster management plan and its sub-
components including the recognition of the evolving risks and
vulnerabilities, and its continued assessment;

b. mainstream those learning experiences relative to the pandemic, into
existing policies and operational processes; and

c. formalize the reduction of vulnerabilities by intensifying information
campaigns intended to raise more public awareness, and have the same
form an integral part of the disaster management plan.

Prepositioning of Equipment and Supplies

Recognizing the initiatives of the CGT, the audit team, in its audit for financial year
2018 documented the names of the partner agencies, organizations and/or private
stakeholders with which the CGT has stand-by agreements with, for the ready and
immediate provision of logistical and immediate needs should a disaster hit the City. That,
during the review, we were then told that the CGT has drawn 21 linkages and partnerships,
and actually had formalized the same by having caused the signing of Memoranda of
Agreement (MOA) with them.

Drawing from that above, the CGT for one has considered as highly important and
necessary those medical supplies and equipment, and food items and expenses intended for
the affected households and front-line personnel. According to the management, the
difficulties in the procurement process have been made manifest by considerable
amendments undertaken against approved and processed purchase orders due to volatility
and limitations of supplies of essential products, materials and goods in the locality as the
pandemic not only has restricted the mobility of delivery vehicles, but as well the travel
restrictions imposed also limited the conduct of canvassing and purchasing from more
locations. All these, according to the management have stalled the efforts for much quicker
responses.

Drawing from the above, the audit team recognizes the limitations brought about
by the COVID-19 pandemic, and that the CGT has taken the extra mile in making the most
of what it has in unlikely situations. However, while MOAs with partner agencies for
logistical support and immediate purchases and delivery had been previously inked, the
audit team is of the view that valuable insights and learning experiences brought about by
the COVID-19 pandemic be made part in the re-drafting of MOAs with these partner
agencies. For one, the previous stand-by agreements have yet to consider factors such as
surge of demand against supplies at the onset of a pandemic, as logically we talked about
of a global phenomenon, and not isolated cases of calamities involving clusters of smaller
locations. Secondly, it is the objective of a well-defined disaster management system and
structure to forge stronger alliances and linkages not only with the local private partner
agencies and firms, but as well as those beyond the jurisdiction of the CGT. In this way,
wider system of support and linkages may be obtained to fill the void once local supplies
for equipment and supplies would not warrant the provisions needed.

We recommended and management agreed to:

a. revisit the MOAs previously inked with the local private partner agencies
and firms, and mainstream the learning experiences brought about by the
COVID-19 pandemic into the agreements to be undertaken with them; and

b. explore forging agreements with private firms and establishments in
nearby localities, or regions as warranted.

Measurement and Reporting of Results

Inherent in the utilization of specially released funds is the expectation of
accountability over the transparent and prudent use of the same. That in the end, resources
are mobilized and utilized as intended. The Bayanihan Fund/Grant takes no exception to
this, as reporting duties are made clear to those responsible thereof under the guidelines.
The concerned LGU officials for that matter are imbued with the utmost duty to see to it
that funds are deployed on the basis of a well-articulated disaster management plan. That
undoubtedly, the disaster plan may have been subjected to several revisions just to suit the
untoward circumstances at hand, but the fact remains that resources out of the one-month
grant have mirrored how the respective LGUs intend to optimize the funds, given the
constraints and the urgency of the situation.

Premised on the above, and in keeping with the Results-Based Management (RBM)
principle, the audit team has drawn management’s attention on what the CGT did at the
instance of planning and/or initiation phase relative to the actions to be taken as responses
to the COVID-19 pandemic. In particular, we asked which parameters were used to
measure or gauge the accomplishments the CGT had insofar as the utilization of the
Bayanihan Fund/Grant is concerned. One comment we gathered manifested that since we
all became strangers to the virus, the LGUs, the CGT included, have accordingly presumed
like everyone else, that one should never let one’s guard down as the situation may not get
any better. It was added that, what concerned the CGT a lot is how to immediately comply
with the directives of the national government, and that being an arm of the latter, the CGT
rushed things up in establishing precautionary measures out of the received Bayanihan
Fund/Grant. That accordingly, what mattered at that time is being able to deliver what
needs to be done at the onset, that is having focused on the output.

On the same vein, another comment obtained, narrated that output, outcome and
impact described results at varying levels. That as continued, outputs are the goods and
services achieved and/or rendered in a development intervention. On one hand, the
outcomes are short to medium term effects of an intervention. While the impacts are those
long-term results. It was further manifested that for purposes of measuring the immediate
successes of the interventions, the outputs are being set as parameters. Accordingly, this is
on top of the reportorial duties mandated by the oversight agencies from the LGUs arising
from the utilization of the Bayanihan Fund/Grant.

The audit team is in agreement of the manifestations made by the management.
That the immediate and logical representation or indicator of the success of any
intervention would be gauging through the output. More so that at the onset of the COVID-
19 pandemic, the LGUs have been left grasping and racing against time. That logically,
one could not easily think of defining which level of results it intends to measure
performance against with, as it is of natural consequence especially when one is hard-
pressed against the time. However, the audit team would like to emphasize the importance
of data management, and reporting thereof for purposes of bridging competencies and
improving performances through being able to analyze data, and incorporating past results
in the crafting of future plan of actions.

To simulate, the audit team would like to illustrate for instance the construction of
isolation facilities as direct outputs sourced against the Bayanihan Fund/Grant. That for
purposes of reporting, the disbursements made would form part of the accountability
reports due the oversight agencies. The same information then becomes a reported
accomplishment as far as the CGT is concerned. The outcome could have zeroed in on, say
the decline of the COVID-19 transmissions due to the immediate confinement of suspected
individuals in appropriate isolation facilities. In this way, the CGT would have a much
wider perspective of how it really fared in terms of the utilization of the funds, and in the
intervention it took. The management however, has yet to elaborate insofar as outcome is
concerned, which should have been the convergence of the effect of the mentioned output
with that of the intended impact. Understandably, we shall not be dealing on the impact as
yet, as the LGUs would have less to no control over it.

We recommended and management agreed that the CGT, within its sphere of
control, consider maximizing its data base in analyzing appropriate levels of results
in keeping with the RBM principle, to enable a more robust reporting mechanism as
valuable basis of drawing informed decisions.

Post Pandemic Strategies

As further enunciated by the management, we came to know of its post pandemic
plan as regards the use of the isolation facility. That said project accordingly, is viewed to
be improved further, and beef up on its equipment through the additional funding
committed by the DILG, DOH and OCD. That as soon as the pandemic is over,
management gave a glimpse of how they would turn the said facility and the improvements
and additional structures thereon into conference centers/rooms, tourism center and the
like, but these all would depend on how the events and the days would unfold in the coming
days, months or years. According to the management, recovery as a community is yet far.
However, the CGT believes that it has the upper hand in forging a fight against this
pandemic. That one key to work on this problem, is to get the cooperation of the people in
the respective localities, and with the right mix of people, and according greater weight in
planning and coordination, things can be done.

Management further explained that the CGT is among those caught empty-handed
by this pandemic. The initial activities undertaken relative to addressing the effects of the
said pandemic were most likely reactive stance, yet the CGT, in the process has gradually
adopted measures aligned with the directives of the national and with that of the provincial
government. But while efforts had been made, experiences have otherwise led the
management to learn the hard way.

We asked the management of things that could best be done, vis-à-vis the
experiences at hand, and we obtained from them that pump priming the economy could
bolster the people’s capacity to earn amidst the pandemic, and offer them sustainable
livelihood opportunities. Another thing is on having the constituents in the locality keep
tab of information. The CGT on the other hand, through the City Health Office (CHO) has
to try beefing up information drive about the ill effects of COVID-19, not to scare the
people away nor to drive them off from their daily tasks, but to usher them to be more
cautious on a day-to-day basis by keeping in mind the safety protocols. As regards the
vaccination plans of the CGT, management said that one common problem that arise is
how the LGUs would be able to fund the procurement of COVID-19 vaccines. According
to the management, this in particular is an issue to those LGUs with meager resources. As
the case in point, the CGT somehow has accordingly set aside an amount for the purpose,
but is however not sufficient to cater to all the constituents of the locality. As mentioned
by the management, they have to actually do more with less. But is however not closing
the possibility of securing loans from banks for the purpose. At the meantime, as continued
by the management, they have to work on prioritizing the health and frontline workers of
the locality, and then directing efforts towards the most vulnerable groups of the locality.

We fully support the herein manifestation made by the management. That in order
to bolster the CGT’s capacity to withstand pandemic of this sort, equal importance has to
be accorded to other facets of the disaster management cycle. That planning has to come
across right from the preparation, prevention, mitigation and up to the rehabilitation stage.
That all of these have to be formally documented including the post pandemic/disaster
strategies.

We recommended and management agreed that the CGT, moving forward,
and in view of the analysis of the results obtained from the interventions undertaken
arising from the use of the Bayanihan Fund/Grant, come up with a rehabilitation
and/or recovery plan to contain and formally document the strategies to be
undertaken after the onslaught of the pandemic.

Recognition of Property, Plant and Equipment Accounts

Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE), as discussed in a separate Audit Observation
Memorandum (AOM) which shall have been issued to your end, are assets that are
expected to be used during more than one reporting period. Its cost includes among others:
a) Its purchase price, including import duties and non-refundable purchase taxes, after
deducting trade discounts and rebates; b) Any costs directly attributable to bringing the
asset to the location and condition necessary for it to be capable of operating in the manner
intended by management23.

Culling on the specific transactions under the Trust Fund account, the audit team
noted of several disbursements especially those that were reported under the account and
lumped as “Other Necessary COVID-19 PPAs & Expenses”. That such transactions
partook those purchases undertaken for equipment and/or materials that, per audit, should
have otherwise formed an integral part of the cost of the building or to more appropriate
accounts. Table D shows the details of the transactions highlighted, viz:

The above-mentioned items highlighted in audit were automatically recorded as

being lodged on the trust account, a separate journal entry should have been prepared to
transfer the said items to the General Fund and be recognized as part of the CGT’s Property,

and thus, recognition of which as assets, as continued by the management, accordingly
were yet to be made. It was further explained that corresponding schedules have already
been prepared on their end for the eventual transfer and recognition. However, according
to the management, due to the numerous transactions and tight work scheduling, the journal
entries for the recognition on the General Fund have yet to be prepared as of this writing.
Taking note of the manifestations propounded by the management, the audit team is
highlighting that the non-recognition of particular items as assets necessarily would, in
effect, understate the CGT’s total assets and equity. That, for purposes of drawing the
corresponding journal entries, reference can be had by revisiting “Annex A” of COA
Circular No. 2012-002 as regards the illustrative journal entries for LDRRM Fund
transactions. Management is in full agreement, and manifested to fast track the actions to
be taken from its end.

We recommended and management agreed that the CGT, through the City
Accounting Office (CAO) to ensure that prior to the closing of books at year-end, to
carefully review transactions under the Trust Fund account. Ascertain whether
disbursements were made for PPEs, and if any, immediately draw corresponding
journal entries to cause the transfer of said purchases to the General Fund account.

Recognition of Inventory Accounts

Inventories are assets in the form of materials or supplies that are: a) consumed in
the production process; b) consumed in the course of agency operations or implementation
of its activities; c) distributed at no charge or for a nominal charge in the pursuit of any
undertaking, programs and projects; d) held for sale in the ordinary course of operations24.
Relatively, and as provided for under the Local Government Accounting Manual (LGAM),
inventories are further classified as a) Inventory held for sale; b) Inventory held for
distribution; c) Inventory held for manufacturing; d) Inventory held for consumption25.
Also, as noted under the CGT’s Notes to the Financial Statements, management made
representations in particular, on its Note 3, Summary of Significant Accounting Policies
that, Inventory is measured at cost upon initial recognition. To the extent that inventory
was received through non-exchange transactions (for no cost or for a nominal cost), the
cost of the inventory is its fair value at the date of acquisition. Also, Note 7 of the same
report stated that the supplies and materials requested and purchased are released to
different offices by the General Services Office (GSO) through the stockroom custodian.
Supplies and materials were not directly charged as expense but rather treated as an
inventory account.

With the premises cited above, and scrutiny of the representation as regards the
LDRRM Fund utilization, vis-à-vis the year-end financial reports submitted to the office,
we noted that the CGT has not reported inventory items, if any, that have remained
unconsumed and/or undistributed as at year-end. In one of the interviews we had, we took
note of the explanations propounded by the management that accordingly, several food
packs stored are made readily available for distribution when needed, and those whose
expiration dates are nearing to an end, are being distributed to various personnel of the
Barangay Peacekeeping Action Teams (BPATs) for their consumption.

We also referred to the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued (SSMI), and that
we observed that Column 4 of the said form, in particular on Requisition and Issue Slip
Numbers/Quantity portion was left unfilled, that any such information obtained thereat
could have been vital in the determination of the movements of supplies requested and
issued. Also, no Report on the Physical Count of Inventory was prepared and submitted.

To continue, the recording of the above-mentioned accounts as outright expenses
upon receipt is a manifestation of a deviation against its adopted accounting policy relative
thereto. There is therefore a possibility of over recording of operating expenditures since
some items accordingly remained in custody as at year end, that ascertaining the same
could not be readily made since there was no conduct of physical count of inventories. On
one hand, in an interview we had, management explained that the CGT does not practice
recognizing inventories as assets. Rather it is accordingly being immediately recorded as
expenses since requested items are directly delivered to the end-users and are not
released/issued from the GSO. That what is being forwarded by the management is that,
they instead have been using the Expense Method in accounting for inventories. The audit
team recognizes the same, but is adamant of the fact that regardless of the method used,
the conduct of physical count of inventory items cannot be dispensed with, as sound
management dictates that controls in the receipt and issuances of inventoriable items
cannot be made subordinate by the method used for the accounting for inventories.

We recommended and management agreed that the CGT, through the
CGSO/Inventory Committee to:

a. institutionalize the inclusion of the conduct of a comprehensive periodic
physical count of unissued inventories by type, at least once a year, and
preferably at year-end before closing of books, and report the same in the
Report of the Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI), and furnish the CAO
for their eventual reconciliation and recording in the books of accounts;

b. consider the formulation of a centralized procurement of goods and services
based on the approved procurement management plan. That, all supplies
and materials for distribution should be requested from and be issued by
the CGSO to enhance accountability thereof.

Further, we also recommended and agreed to by the management that the
CGT, through the CAO to draw corresponding journal entries to adjust the recorded
expenses and to account for supplies on hand as at year end in reference to the RPCI
prepared by the CGSO/Inventory Committee.

Charging of Expenses against the LDRRM Continuing Fund

Unexpended/unobligated balance of the LDRRM fund for Capital Outlay shall
remain in the General Fund books as continuing appropriations until the projects funded
thereto are completed and any savings shall be available for use in the LDRRM activities
as provided in the Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Fund Investment Plan
(LDRRMFIP)26. Relatively, Capital Outlay or capital expenditures, as defined under
Section 306 (d) of the Local Government Code of 1991 (R.A. No. 7160), are appropriations
spent for the purchase of goods or services, the benefits of which extend beyond the fiscal
year and which add to the assets of the LGU concerned.

Referencing back on Table C earlier, we noted that the CGT had utilized portion of
its continuing fund amounting to ₱629,900.00 for COVID-19 related expenses. Also, the
charges against the said fund partook the nature of maintenance and other operating
expenses rather than capital expenditures, contrary to the definition and guidelines
provided for by the rules with respect to the use of continuing funds and/or appropriations,
which ideally should have been confined to capital expenditures that transcends beyond
the budget year.

We recommended and management agreed that the CGT, in coordination
with the CAO, and that of the City Budget Office (CBO), to revisit the guidelines as
regards the rules on the use of the funds to ensure better appreciation of the reporting
mechanisms.

Representations of Management as Contained in the Submitted Reports

A high-quality financial report possesses reliability on the presented financial
information. The reliability principle is plain and simple, information can be checked,
verified, and reviewed with objective evidence. However, despite its simplicity, this
concept is of high importance especially for the users of the financial report. Thus, to
support the required presentation of information as regards the utilization of the LDRRM
Fund, COA Circular No. 2012-002 provides that the amount and details of the unexpended
balance of the LDRRM Fund shall be discussed in the Notes to the Financial Statements.
With that end, and as part of the CGT’s accountability towards public funds, it has
prepared its C.Y. 2020 annual year-end reports which include among others the Notes to
the Financial Statements. As compliance with the Full Disclosure Policy of the Department

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that Management should direct the officials concerned to ensure that the 20% of the IRA is optimally utilized in the prompt implementation of programs and projects to achieve the desirable socio-economic development and as prescribed under DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-01.

Moreover, Management should enhance the full rate of implementation of projects funded from 20% Development Fund so that the desired benefits and services which go with the projects are delivered to the constituents of the City without delay.

Further, Management should improve the project monitoring and evaluation system which has the potential of mitigating delays in project implementation arising from those mentioned factors.
observations.managementAction:
Unimplemented projects for 20% Development Fund -2016 were implemented the following Year 2017. Balance as of December 31, 217 amounted to P13,901,769.25 or equivalent to 14% unimplemented.

We have obligated P6,000,000.00 worth of project for 2018 and the balance of P7,901,769.25 will be for implementation this year.

We have establish a system of implementation by authorizing the City Planning and Development Office to prepare program of works for SEF and barangay projects. Management will provide Software, equipment and manpower complement to help Engineering in project implementation.

Other offices will help in monitoring the implementation of projects.
observations.reason:
The City Government is presently constrained from accomplishing much in view of the inadequacy of the manpower complement. 

However, a system is planned to be establish to somehow improve on its performance, including tapping other offices for any assistance.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to:
Enhance the administration and collection of SEF through adopting a more realistic and current valuation of properties and intensive collection of delinquent accounts; and
Improve the processes of planning, prioritization and budgeting of SEF through:
Instituting measures to make the LSB more proactive and effective in introducing educational reforms within its capacity;
The formulation of development programs on education for the City Government, articulating the collective vision of the major stakeholders;
Increasing participation and involvement of various sectors, including but not limited to parents, teachers, non-teaching personnel, and among others, in the regular consultative meetings of the LSB;
Strictly requiring the submission of the respective schools’ budget proposals together with their annual appropriations from the national government to be able to properly identify the items for funding augmentation; and
The creation of an ad hoc Technical Working Group (TWG) to spearhead the evaluation and prioritization of the submitted proposals.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Develop and implement measures to make the processes of disbursements more transparent by adhering as much as possible to the law, rules and regulations; and
Refrain from accommodating expenditures which do not fall within the recommended programs, activities and projects chargeable against the SEF.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Enhance accountability of the acquired and issued assets and/or properties by conducting regular physical inventory of the same, and evaluating on a timely basis the status and issuance of these subject assets to monitor their whereabouts and serviceability; and
Revisit the relevant guidelines on SEF and related rules and regulations on property, supply and inventory management for the proper disposition and issuance of LGU procured assets;

We further recommended and Management likewise agreed to:
Exert diligent efforts in locating the documents pertaining to the unreconciled and unaccounted asset balances to be able to properly reflect the accounts at its true value.

We recommended and Management agreed to hasten the development of measures, within its capacity, to assess projects, activities and programs with respect not only to the desired outputs, but as well to the outcomes and impacts these projects have to the locality.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Make representation with the EMB of the DENR Regional Office No. 12, together with the NSWMC as regards the status of the approval of the City Government’s SWMP;
Revisit, evaluate and assess the City Government’s presently adopted SWMP and have the same aligned with that of the one approved by the authority; and
Regularly practice the earlier drafting, preparation and submission of the SWMP, with a view of the previously obtained feedbacks and/or comments from the concerned approving authority.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Evaluate the reliability of the perimeter enclosures, including the gates. Draw necessary plans lay-outing how the enclosures be improved and be made sturdy and stable to be able to restrict access therefrom;
Strictly limit access to the premises by making necessary logs of those who were allowed entry each day in order to have a document to refer to, should there be accidents or any untoward events that may transpire within the site; and
Draft policies and/or guidelines on the wearing of protective gears and equipment for visitors, wastes pickers/scavengers and personnel whenever they are within the vicinity.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Conduct inventory of unserviceable vehicles owned by the City including those kept in the dump site, and other areas in preparation for their proper disposal. As soon as the inventory has been completed, assess the possibilities on how the disposals shall be made;
Prepare a report documenting the items that may no longer be disposed through sale, and are considered waste. Explore options on how these wastes be disposed of properly; and
Strengthen the measures in ensuring the conduct of information drive/campaign regarding the proper management of solid wastes; the effects and/or impacts it bring to the quality of life.

We recommended that the Management:

pre-terminate the time deposit account totaling P43,777,311.87 and transfer back these account to the Trust Fund Cash in Bank – Local Currency, Current Account;

determine the details and specific purpose of the said funds invested in time deposit account and immediately implement the same; and

in case/s of unexpended balance and savings from implemented projects from Trust Fund, coordinate with the grantor agencies of the fund and return the balance, if the grantor is no longer in the service/or cannot be determined, deposit the amount directly to the Bureau of the Treasury.

We recommended that the management require the Inventory Committee to complete the inventory taking of the property and equipment through the preparation of well-accomplished Report on Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RCPPE) and submit the same to the Audit Team as required by Sec 124 of NGAS Manual Volume I.

Also, we recommended that the GSO and Accounting Office conduct regular reconciliation of the PPE records they maintain, verify and analyze the discrepancies, and promptly adjust when appropriate.

observations.managementAction: Retrieval of Public Infrastructures from the Registry of Public Infrastructures to the Annual Financial report were made after conversion of NGAs to PPSAS.
observations.reason:
As of year 2017 report, we have computed Depreciation Expense from 2010 to 2017 or 8 years.

We still have 145 months or 12 years to charge depreciation expense for a 20 year life span for road inventory.

observations.recommendation: We recommended for the verification of the balances of the accounts under Trust Funds. Idle trust funds should immediately be utilized in accordance with the purpose for which the funds was granted. While the excess funds of completed or implemented projects should immediately be returned to its grantor/s, and if the grantor is no longer in the service/or cannot be determined, deposit the amount directly to the Bureau of the Treasury.
We recommended that the City return all the unutilized/unexpended PDAF to the Bureau of Treasury for its reversion to the general fund.

We recommended that the Accounting Office maintain a separate special account in the General Fund for the 20% Development Fund and the economic enterprises the LGU operates and consequently prepare separate trial balances and financial statements reflecting the financial condition and results of their respective operations, in accordance with existing regulations.

We recommended that the City Accountant reconcile the reciprocal accounts and determine the cause/s of the discrepancy in the balances,  and make appropriate adjustments, if warranted, for fair presentation in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Ongoing efforts are exerted by the Management to fully implement the said recommendation.
observations.reason:
The discrepancy in balances particularly the SET Receivables and Deferred SET Income amounting to P2,496,303.69 was adjusted per JV# 010002 dated January 27, 2017 copy of the Journal is hereto attached.

Due to Other Funds amounting to P9,393.39 are withholding taxes as of November 28, 2016 and December 13, 2016 amounting to P852.60 and P8,540.76 respectively.

Withholding taxes of the local Government Unit are remitted to the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) through the General Fund Account by means of online remittance through Electronic Filing Payment System (EFPS).

Actual Remittances of Taxes for SEF and trust fund accounts are settled by transferring the corresponding amounts to the General Fund the following month. These causes the discrepancy in the accounts. The amount of P9,393.39 was settled January 4,2017 as per DV# 0100006.

The amount of P1,702,500 represents collection/ receipt of funds intended for the Social Pension under Trust Fund dated November 29, 2016 as per RCD# C1100047 but deposited to General Fund account.

Transfer of fund from General Fund to Trust Fund was made on January 26, 2017 with authority to Debit Account.
